cards.back: A group of zero or more statements treated by the compiler as a single statement. Also called a block or block statement.

cards.text: Blocks begin with a {{c1::{}} symbol and end with a {{c2::}}} symbol. {{c3::No semicolon}} is needed at the end of a block.

cards.front: Can functions be nested inside other functions in C++?
cards.back: No. Although functions cannot be nested inside other functions, blocks can be nested inside other blocks.

cards.text: When blocks are nested, the enclosing block is called the {{c1::outer block}} and the enclosed block is called the {{c2::inner block}} or {{c3::nested block}}.

cards.front: What is the most common use case for blocks in C++?
cards.back: In conjunction with if statements, to execute multiple statements conditionally when a condition evaluates to true.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Blocks should not have a semicolon after the closing brace.
cards.explanation: Unlike statements, blocks do not require a semicolon at the end. The semicolon after the closing brace is unnecessary and can be confusing.

cards.back: The maximum number of nested blocks you can be inside at any point in a function, including the outer block.

cards.question: What is the nesting level of this function?
cards.explanation: The nesting level is 3 because the deepest point in the function has 3 nested blocks (the outer main block, the first if block, and the nested if block).

cards.text:
A function body is a {{c1::block}}. Example:
int add(int x, int y)
{{c2::{}} // start block
    return x + y;
{{c3::}}} // end block (no semicolon)

cards.front: What is the recommended maximum nesting level for functions?
cards.back: 3 or less. Functions with more nested levels should be refactored into sub-functions.

cards.front: How many levels of nesting should C++ compilers support according to the C++ standard?
cards.back: 256 levels of nesting (though not all compilers support this many, e.g., Visual Studio supports fewer).

cards.concept: Why keep nesting levels to 3 or less
cards.explanation: Overly-nested blocks are hard to read and understand. Just as overly-long functions should be refactored into smaller functions, deeply-nested blocks should be refactored with the most-nested blocks becoming separate functions.

cards.text: To execute multiple statements conditionally with if, use a {{c1::block}} enclosed in {{c2::{} braces}}.
A function body is an example of a {{c1::block}}, starting with {{c2::{}} and ending with {{c3::}}}.
The recommended maximum {{c1::nesting level}} for functions is {{c2::3}} or less to maintain code readability.
